title: A sea-oriented economy before oil
content: Long before petroleum, Norway developed fisheries, shipbuilding, and shipping networks. Maritime capabilities formed a durable economic base and global orientation.

title: Hydropower and industry expanded capacity
content: Abundant water resources supported hydropower, enabling energy-intensive industries. This linked geography to industrial competitiveness.
next: Then offshore energy transformed the scale.

title: Oil and gas changed national income
content: North Sea discoveries dramatically increased export revenues and state fiscal power. Petroleum became central to growth, jobs, and external balance.
next: Resource wealth can still be risky.

title: Norway tried to avoid the resource curse
content: Instead of spending all oil revenue directly, Norway built fiscal rules and a sovereign wealth fund to smooth cycles and preserve value for future generations.
next: The economy is not only oil, though.

title: Services, technology, and ocean industries matter
content: Norway combines resource sectors with advanced maritime services, engineering, aquaculture, digital industries, and high-skill labor markets.

title: A small open economy tied to world markets
content: Exchange rates, commodity prices, and European demand strongly affect Norway. Economic policy therefore focuses on resilience and productivity, not isolation.
next: The transition question is now central.

title: Energy transition is both risk and opportunity
content: Climate policy and energy transition challenge petroleum dependence while opening opportunities in green shipping, offshore wind, and low-carbon technologies.
next: The long-term model depends on adaptation.

title: Wealth came from resources plus institutions
content: Norway's prosperity is not explained by oil alone. It depends on governance quality, human capital, social trust, and disciplined long-term fiscal strategy.
next: This links directly back to politics and society.
